informal: Suppose $f^{\prime}(x)>0$ in $(a, b)$. Prove that $f$ is strictly increasing in $(a, b)$, and let $g$ be its inverse function. Prove that $g$ is differentiable, and that $g^{\prime}(f(x))=\frac{1}{f^{\prime}(x)} \quad(a < x < b)$.
lean:
theorem Rudin_exercise_5_2_corrected :
    ∀ (a b : ℝ) (f g : ℝ → ℝ),
      (∀ x ∈ Set.Ioo a b, deriv f x > 0) →
      (∀ x ∈ Set.Ioo a b, g (f x) = x) →
      (∀ x ∈ Set.Ioo a b, ContinuousAt g (f x)) →
      (∀ x ∈ Set.Ioo a b, ∀ᶠ y in 𝓝 (f x), f (g y) = y) →
      StrictMonoOn f (Set.Ioo a b) ∧
        DifferentiableOn ℝ g (f '' Set.Ioo a b) ∧
          ∀ x ∈ Set.Ioo a b, deriv g (f x) = 1 / deriv f x
reasoning: The informal statement only assumes f'(x) > 0 on (a,b); from this one must derive that f is strictly increasing, that an inverse g exists, that g is continuous, and that g is differentiable with the stated derivative. The Lean signature instead takes as additional hypotheses that g is a left inverse of f on (a,b), that g is continuous at each f(x), and that f∘g equals the identity in a neighborhood of f(x) (a local right-inverse condition). These continuity and inverse-function facts are precisely what the informal exercise asks the prover to establish; assuming them turns the theorem into a substantially easier claim. Hence the Lean version is implied by the informal statement (under those assumptions the conclusions follow) but does not imply it, so it is logically weaker.
